data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: setForNextEpoch requires GOVERNANCE, calls _requireInitialized(), then checks only `if (_epochIntervalMicros == 0) revert InvalidEpochInterval()`. No upper-bound check. type(uint64).max (18446744073709551615) is accepted and stored to _pendingEpochIntervalMicros; hasPendingConfig = true.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: caller must be SystemAddresses.GOVERNANCE; contract must be initialized

data_flow_trace.original: Pending value stored: _pendingEpochIntervalMicros = type(uint64).max, hasPendingConfig = true
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: Assignment is a plain store of the uint64 value with no sanitation; emits PendingEpochIntervalSet.

data_flow_trace.original: At next epoch boundary, Reconfiguration._applyReconfiguration() calls EpochConfig.applyPendingConfig() — sets epochIntervalMicros = type(uint64).max
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: _applyReconfiguration() invokes EpochConfig(...).applyPendingConfig() at line 274. applyPendingConfig copies _pendingEpochIntervalMicros into epochIntervalMicros unconditionally (no range check), clears hasPendingConfig, emits events.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: applyPendingConfig is RECONFIGURATION-only; only runs via _applyReconfiguration
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: line number is 274 (not 267 as issue stated) but semantics identical

data_flow_trace.original: Same transaction resets lastReconfigurationTime = ITimestamp.nowMicroseconds(), sets _transitionState = Idle
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: After applying configs, _applyReconfiguration sets currentEpoch = newEpoch, lastReconfigurationTime = ITimestamp.nowMicroseconds(), _transitionState = TransitionState.Idle.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: reached inside the same tx that applied the pending config

data_flow_trace.original: On the very next block, Blocker.onBlockStart() calls checkAndStartTransition()
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: onBlockStart (called by SYSTEM_CALLER at block prologue) unconditionally invokes IReconfiguration(RECONFIGURATION).checkAndStartTransition() on every block after timestamp update. No try/catch wrapping, so any revert bubbles up and aborts the prologue.

data_flow_trace.original: checkAndStartTransition() → state == Idle → falls through to _canTransition()
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: checkAndStartTransition first returns if _transitionState == DkgInProgress; otherwise it calls `if (!_canTransition()) return false;`. With state Idle, _canTransition is invoked.

data_flow_trace.original: _canTransition(): lastReconfigurationTime + epochInterval overflows uint64 under 0.8 checked arithmetic → reverts
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: Function body: `uint64 currentTime = ITimestamp(...).nowMicroseconds(); uint64 epochInterval = EpochConfig(...).epochIntervalMicros(); return currentTime >= lastReconfigurationTime + epochInterval;`. With epochInterval = type(uint64).max and lastReconfigurationTime > 0 (set in step 4 to real microsecond time ~1.75e15), the addition overflows uint64 and Solidity 0.8.30 checked math reverts with Panic(0x11).
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: lastReconfigurationTime >= 1 (guaranteed: set to nowMicroseconds at initialize and every apply)
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: none — bug is present exactly as described

data_flow_trace.original: Revert propagates up through checkAndStartTransition() → Blocker.onBlockStart() — block prologue fails, chain halts
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: Blocker.onBlockStart has no try/catch around checkAndStartTransition (ComponentUpdateFailed event exists for other components but is not used here). A revert inside _canTransition propagates to checkAndStartTransition, then to onBlockStart, aborting the block prologue. Every subsequent block's prologue hits the same overflow, halting block production.

reasoning_chain: The issue describes a path from governance input to chain halt via uint64 overflow inside Blocker's block prologue. I traced every step on main@a623eab. (1) setForNextEpoch only rejects zero, accepting any uint64 including type(uint64).max. (2) The pending value is stored verbatim. (3) applyPendingConfig copies the pending value into epochIntervalMicros with no range validation. (4) _applyReconfiguration resets lastReconfigurationTime to the current microsecond timestamp (a large positive number on any live chain). (5) The very next block's Blocker.onBlockStart unconditionally calls checkAndStartTransition — no try/catch. (6) With _transitionState == Idle, _canTransition is executed. (7) The expression `lastReconfigurationTime + epochInterval` performs checked uint64 addition; lastReconfigurationTime is non-zero microsecond time and epochInterval == 2^64-1, which overflows and reverts under Solidity 0.8.30 checked arithmetic. (8) The revert bubbles all the way out of onBlockStart, aborting every block prologue on each subsequent slot — a chain halt. No upper-bound validation exists in setForNextEpoch, applyPendingConfig, or initialize; Grep across src/runtime/EpochConfig.sol and src/foundation/Errors.sol confirms only InvalidEpochInterval (the != 0 check). No saturating arithmetic or unchecked block wraps the _canTransition addition. There are no regression tests in test/unit/runtime/EpochConfig.t.sol or test/unit/blocker/Reconfiguration.t.sol that exercise type(uint64).max or overflow. Recovery via governanceReconfigure() (Reconfiguration.sol:141-166) bypasses _canTransition, but governance transactions themselves require working blocks — since onBlockStart reverts every block, normal governance execution paths are also blocked, amplifying severity.

key_evidence.excerpt: function setForNextEpoch(uint64 _epochIntervalMicros) external { ... if (_epochIntervalMicros == 0) { revert Errors.InvalidEpochInterval(); } _pendingEpochIntervalMicros = _epochIntervalMicros; hasPendingConfig = true; ... }
key_evidence.why_matters: Only validation is `!= 0`; no upper bound. type(uint64).max is an accepted input.

key_evidence.excerpt: function applyPendingConfig() external { requireAllowed(RECONFIGURATION); ... epochIntervalMicros = _pendingEpochIntervalMicros; hasPendingConfig = false; ... }
key_evidence.why_matters: Unconditional copy — no sanitization at apply time either.

key_evidence.excerpt: function _canTransition() internal view returns (bool) { uint64 currentTime = ITimestamp(...).nowMicroseconds(); uint64 epochInterval = EpochConfig(...).epochIntervalMicros(); return currentTime >= lastReconfigurationTime + epochInterval; }
key_evidence.why_matters: Checked uint64 addition with attacker-controllable epochInterval; overflows whenever lastReconfigurationTime + epochInterval > 2^64-1.
No try/catch — any revert in _canTransition propagates and aborts onBlockStart, i.e. the block prologue.

key_evidence.excerpt: uint64 newEpoch = currentEpoch + 1; currentEpoch = newEpoch; lastReconfigurationTime = ITimestamp(...).nowMicroseconds(); _transitionState = TransitionState.Idle;
key_evidence.why_matters: lastReconfigurationTime is set to a real microsecond timestamp (non-zero, ~1.75e15 currently), guaranteeing the subsequent addition with 2^64-1 overflows.

counterargument: One might argue the overflow is not exploitable because: (a) governance itself is the attacker, so this is a trusted-privilege scenario rather than a vulnerability — governance could just as easily set any malicious value; (b) recovery is possible via governanceReconfigure() which bypasses _canTransition() (Reconfiguration.sol:141-166). Rebuttal: (a) Gravity's threat model explicitly treats governance bugs as bugs when they can produce unrecoverable chain state — issue #403 (lockupDurationMicros overflow) is already accepted under the same threat model, and a simple input sanity check (`epochIntervalMicros <= MAX_REASONABLE_INTERVAL`) would close both a misconfiguration footgun and an adversarial-governance surface. (b) governanceReconfigure() is itself a transaction; transactions require block production; block production invokes Blocker.onBlockStart which unconditionally calls checkAndStartTransition which reverts. Unless the node operator ships an emergency patch that skips onBlockStart or the SYSTEM_CALLER can submit transactions out-of-band, normal governance recovery is impossible — the chain is effectively bricked until an off-chain hard-fork. This matches the issue's impact claim.
